text: This is an educational video for surgeons describing a laparoscopic repair of a Peterson's defect for acute bowel obstruction with chylosis ascites. The patient was a fifty-six-year-old man who presented with acute abdominal pain. One year prior, he underwent a laparoscopic gastric bypass. The roux limb was constructed in an anticholic-antigastric position. Initial health and weight loss were excellent. On physical exam, he was tender in the left hemiabdomen. His laboratory findings were unremarkable. CT scan showed a small bowel feces sign, fluid in the mesenteric leaflets, and swirled mesenteric vessels. We suspected an internal hernia and recommended diagnostic laparoscopy.

Ports are placed in the right hemiabdomen, with the surgeon positioned on the patient's right side. During diagnostic laparoscopy, Kyle pooling is observed within the abdomen and between the mesenteric leaflets. The surgeon identifies the leaf of Treves and the terminal ileum, facilitating the reduction of the small intestine and identification of the internal hernia.

The surgeon reduces the small intestine, revealing lymph trapped within the mesenteric leaflets and venous congestion. The small bowel is reduced up to the gastrojejunostomy. The surgeon systematically searches for internal hernias, checking the jejunojejunostomy and mesenteric leaflets, finding a sutured hernia. The Roux limb is flipped to the left to identify a large mesenteric defect, a Peterson's hernia, causing bowel obstruction. The defect is sutured closed using non-absorbable suture.

The surgeon completes the closure of the Peterson's defect using sutures. The bowel is visually inspected once more to confirm the absence of additional abnormalities. The ascitic fluid appears clear, and the patient is prepared for discharge.
